[JS Weird World] No.63 Building Our Own Framework: The Requirements
Now that we've got a rough handle on jQuery from the earlier chapters, the natural next step is to actually get our hands dirty and build a little framework of our own.

We can use everything we've learned so far to put together our own mini framework — and this one is actually usable in real projects too. But before anything else, we should give our framework a name.
We're building something simple and lightweight here, so just like the course, we'll call it GREETR. So what should it do?
When you log in, it shows a "Hello".
If you give it a name, it can give you a formal greeting and an informal one.
And even though it's called GREETR, when you actually call it you use
On top of that, GREETR has to support jQuery — it returns greetings, but under the hood it's really returning jQuery. So those are the requirements we're going to build toward.

Now that we've got a rough handle on jQuery, it's time to roll up our sleeves and build a little framework of our own. Before we write any code, let's lay out exactly what GREETR needs to do — the greetings, the language support, and the jQuery integration we're aiming for.
